S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Name each type of access Cowork can use and describe its risk profile.
· Choose the narrowest access method appropriate to a given task.
· Write an access-review prompt before starting a Cowork task.
· Know when to refuse a connector or plugin because its scope is too wide.
· Treat permissions as a routine design step, not an afterthought.
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: OPENING SCENE
blocks: Mariana works in grants management at a regional nonprofit. Tuesday morning she has a deadline: a funder wants a summary
Opening Scene — She does not stop to think about what "connect Google Drive" means in practice. The connector gives Cowork access to eve

data.title: THE ACCESS LADDER.
blocks: Uploaded/task files — Bounded source packet for a single task
Local folder — Multi-file work from a prepared workspace
Cloud connector — Structured access to a specific service
Browser assistance — Public web interaction, reference lookups

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — EACH ACCESS TYPE IN PLAIN TERM

data.title: EACH ACCESS TYPE IN PLAIN TERMS.
blocks: The most controlled access. You choose specific files, attach them to a Cowork session, and those files become the worki
Each Access Type in Plain Terms — Use this for: one-time tasks with a clear source set. Prepare the files first (copies, not originals), attach them, and
data.section: EACH ACCESS TYPE IN PLAIN TERM

S06.meta: // SLIDE 06 — THE PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILE

data.title: THE PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILEGE.
blocks: · What source does this task actually need?
· Can I use uploaded files instead of a folder or connector?
· If a connector, can it be scoped to a folder or project rather than the full account?
· Is browser access necessary, or can the information come from uploaded sources?
· Is computer use necessary, or is there a connector that handles this?

S07.meta: // SLIDE 07 — ACCESS REVIEW: A PROMPT TO USE

data.title: ACCESS REVIEW: A PROMPT TO USE
blocks: Before starting a task, ask Cowork to declare its access requirements. Paste this into your session:
Access Review: A Prompt to Use — "Before we begin: list exactly what access you need for this task, why each access type is necessary, what you will not
data.section: ACCESS REVIEW: A PROMPT TO USE

S08.meta: // SLIDE 08 — THE HUMAN GATE FOR THIS CHAPTE

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE FOR THIS CHAPTER.
blocks: · What is the smallest access surface that still lets the task succeed?
· Have I scoped this to copies, a prepared folder, or a limited connector rather than my full account?
· Do I understand what actions are enabled by the access I am granting?
· Am I comfortable with what Cowork could do if it makes a reasonable error?
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE FOR THIS CHAPTE

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Connecting the full account because it is faster.
Assuming read-only access is safe.
Enabling a plugin without reviewing what it adds.
Using browser access for tasks that uploaded files would cover.
Scheduling high-stakes tasks without an approval gate.

data.title: BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 4.
blocks: You now know what Cowork can access and how to limit it. The next question is: what do you actually ask Cowork to do?
